coverage_map.coverage_assessment: The dataset reveals a highly restricted, foundational scope limited to single-step isolated rule application. It tests logarithmic quotient/reciprocal/power rules, conceptual magnitude estimation, and single-step exponential base manipulations. There is absolutely no coverage of multi-step equations, systems, base-change formulas applied to complex expressions, or logarithmic domain restrictions. The topic acts as a check on basic symbolic literacy and operational definitions rather than rigorous algebraic problem-solving.

difficulty_ladder.note: Total Easy bias: all 7 questions are Easy. Both Medium and Hard blueprints are fully extrapolated. Zero data-grounded examples exist for either tier.

easy.concept_mixing: None. Each question isolates a single rule or concept.
easy.qualifier_sensitivity: Low. Variables are given basic constraints ('greater than 1') solely to ensure mathematical validity.
easy.answer_similarity: Distinct values engineered to match the exact output of misapplied logarithmic/exponential rules.

medium.steps_required: 2 to 3 steps, requiring initial manipulation before rule application
medium.concept_mixing: Mixed log + exponential switching within a single equation (e.g., evaluating 2*log_2(x) + log_3(9x))
medium.qualifier_sensitivity: Moderate. Introduction of log domain constraints (e.g., x>0) requiring active checking.
medium.answer_similarity: Options explicitly include extraneous roots generated by squaring arguments or combining logarithms, penalizing failure to check domains.

hard.steps_required: 4+ steps, requiring extensive base unification and substitution
hard.concept_mixing: Base change ambiguity (e.g., equations mixing log_2(x), log_4(x), and log_16(x) requiring harmonization)
hard.qualifier_sensitivity: High. Ambiguous variable relationships where hidden equivalences depend entirely on unstated or subtle sign constraints.
hard.answer_similarity: Competing valid transformations — options present the same mathematical value in different disguised base/exponent forms, forcing deep property knowledge to verify equivalence.

traps.name: Reciprocal Inversion Confusion
traps.mechanism: Confusing the mathematical reciprocal (1/x) with a sign flip (-x) or an inverse operation (√x).
traps.exact_values: Given log_a(b)=3, correct is 1/3, traps are -3 and √3

traps.name: Sign-of-Exponent / Fractional Base Trap
traps.mechanism: Evaluating logarithms with fractional bases resulting in integers >1, failing to account for the negative exponent required for base inversion.
traps.exact_values: Given log_(1/3)(9), correct is -2, traps drop the sign (2) or invert the exponent (1/2, -1/2)

traps.name: Power-Rule Doubling Confusion
traps.mechanism: Given log_a(b)=k and asked for log_a(b^n), applying the exponent n to the log value k incorrectly — exponentiating instead of multiplying, or dividing instead of multiplying.
traps.exact_values: Correct = n*k = 8. Traps: k^n = 16 (power error), k/n = 2 (division error), 1/k = 1/4 (full inversion)

traps.name: Forced x-Solving Trap
traps.mechanism: Given (c^m)^x = b and asked for c^x, students waste time solving for x rather than executing direct root extraction (b^(1/m)).

traps.name: Insufficient Data Meta-Trap
traps.mechanism: Abstract variables (a, b) trigger insecurity, prompting 'cannot be determined' selections despite universal identity applicability.

M1Q11.A: Base/argument confusion — switched to natural log of the given value: ln(3)
M1Q11.B: Meta-trap — falsely believes abstract variables make the identity undeterminable
M1Q11.C: False root transformation — took square root of the given value: √3
M1Q11.D: False sign-flip transformation — negated the given value: -3

M1Q44.A: Polynomial intersection assumption — assumed 4 crossing points like degree-4 curves
M1Q44.B: Polynomial intersection assumption — assumed 2 crossing points
M1Q44.D: Polynomial intersection assumption — assumed 3 crossing points
M1Q44.E: Different-base divergence assumption — assumed 2^x and 3^x never meet

M2Q5.D: Subtractive argument error — computed log(6-3) = log(3)
M2Q5.E: Unlisted structural error — assumed result is not among standard log values

M2Q44.A: Dropped negative sign from base inversion — answered +2 instead of -2
M2Q44.B: Reciprocal exponent error with sign — answered -1/2
M2Q44.C: Reciprocal exponent + dropped sign error — answered +1/2
M2Q44.D: False base-extraction identity — returned base with negative sign: -1/3

M3Q3.B: Base-exponent collision — returned b^x (10^9) instead of x (9)
M3Q3.C: Base collision — returned the base itself (10) instead of the exponent
M3Q3.E: Surface string parsing — returned leading digit cluster (999)

M4Q21.B: Meta-trap — falsely believes abstract variables make the power rule undeterminable

time_pressure_anatomy.description: Cognitive load is purely structural. Time is lost at the mode-switching boundary between logarithmic and exponential representations. Because visual similarity between logarithm rules is high, verification cost under stress is severe — students attempting to check answers by substituting test numbers into abstract forms bleed seconds. The Forced x-Solving trap (M4Q15) specifically weaponizes algorithmic defaults by prompting a lengthy calculation-heavy algorithm (isolating x) when a 3-second structural shortcut ((3^x)^2=16) is intended.

mechanisms.name: Log/Exp Mode-Switching Boundary
mechanisms.description: Translating between logarithmic and exponential representations requires a mental register switch that is error-prone under time pressure.

mechanisms.name: Symbol Verification Cost
mechanisms.description: Checking answers by substituting test numbers into abstract log expressions is slow. Subscripts, superscripts, and fraction bars require careful re-reading, compounding verification time.

mechanisms.name: Algorithmic Default Trap
mechanisms.description: M4Q15 triggers the default 'solve for x' algorithm when the intended path is direct structural recognition. Students who default to the algorithm waste time and may reach a non-integer x before realising the question does not ask for x.

calibrated_uplift.key_principle: Bocconi difficulty = familiar problem + evil answer choices + slight ambiguity + time pressure. NOT harder algebra.
calibrated_uplift.layer_1_problem_structure_mutation: Shift from isolated evaluations to expressions requiring initial base unification (e.g., log_2(x) + log_4(x)).
calibrated_uplift.layer_2_cognitive_load_injection: Add abstract base constraints (0<a<1) that reverse standard inequality evaluations.
calibrated_uplift.layer_3_answer_engineering: MOST WEIGHT. Deterministic rule-based distractor engine. For logarithmic power/reciprocal rules (input: log_a(b)=k, target operator n): correct = n*k or 1/k; distractors must output exactly k^n (power instead of multiply), k/n (divide instead of multiply), k+n (add instead of multiply), -k (sign flip instead of reciprocal). For exponential base reductions (input: (c^m)^x=b, target: c^x): correct = b^(1/m); distractors must output b^m (inverted operation), b^(1/2m) (double root), c (target base collision), c^m (original base collision). For logarithmic combinations (input: log(A) ± log(B)): correct = log(A*B) or log(A/B); distractors must output log(A±B) (operator mirroring), log(A)*log(B) (false distribution).

figure_passage_interaction_patterns.note: 0 of 7 questions have figures. Symbolic notation itself is the primary source of misreading risk.
symbolic_risk_patterns: Structural conflation of log_a(b^n) with (log_a(b))^n — the first multiplies the log value by n, the second exponentiates it
Fractional bases (e.g., 1/3) induce visual skipping — students process the '3', solve the power relationship, and fail to register the fraction bar that necessitates a negative exponent
Subscript base confusion — log_a(b) vs log_b(a) are visually similar but mathematically reciprocal

ambiguity_patterns.primary_ambiguity_mechanism: The 'Insufficient Data' meta-trap. By refusing to provide numbers for a and b, the test manufactures artificial doubt about whether logarithmic identities are absolute. This is the only source of genuine ambiguity in the topic — all other difficulty comes from deterministic rule misapplication.
ambiguity_patterns.near_correct_construction: Options are mechanically coupled — they do not represent random arithmetic errors but the exact numeric output of specific, formalized structural misinterpretations (e.g., k^n vs n*k).

generation_fingerprint:
## LOGARITHMS/EXPONENTIALS GENERATION FINGERPRINT

The Bocconi Logarithms/Exponentials dataset tests structural recognition over algorithmic depth, exhibiting a total Easy bias (7/7 Easy). The primary difficulty driver is the exact mechanical mapping of student transformation errors into the answer choices, not logarithm complexity. Generators must not default to generic hard algebra — Medium and Hard levels must be engineered by introducing base change ambiguity, mixed log/exp mode-switching, and extraneous root generation from domain constraints.

Question generation requires a deterministic distractor engine based on exact transformation errors. The four primary high-leverage mechanics are:

The Reciprocal Inversion Trap (M1Q11 pattern): Given log_a(b)=k, distractors must output the sign flip (-k), root (√k), and natural log (ln(k)) rather than the true reciprocal (1/k).

The Sign-of-Exponent Trap (M2Q44 pattern): For fractional bases evaluating to integers, distractors must explicitly output the dropped sign (|x|), the reciprocal exponent (1/x), and the base-extraction identity.

The Power-Rule Engine (M4Q21 pattern): Given log_a(b)=k, evaluating log_a(b^n) must generate a distractor set populated strictly by k^n (power error) and k/n (division error) against the correct n*k.

The Exponential Base Reduction Engine (M4Q15 pattern): Given (c^m)^x=b, finding c^x must generate distractors based on inverted operations (b^m) and base collisions (c or c^m), penalizing students who attempt to solve for x.

The 'Insufficient Data' meta-trap must frequently anchor distractor sets for equations using abstract variables. General random-error distractors are strictly forbidden. Every option must be an engineered, traceable trap.

Adhere to the Elimination Mapping Rule: every wrong option must correspond to a specific traceable student error — the generator must be able to state exactly which mistake leads to selecting each distractor.
